content:
# Luke Chapter 6 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 91-93 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_006_page_001.jpg, LUK_006_page_002.jpg, LUK_006_page_003.jpg

---

**1.** And it came-to-pass on a sabbath, he was going-through the grain-fields, and his disciples were plucking and eating the ears-of-grain, rubbing [them] with [their] hands.

**2.** And certain of the Pharisees said, "Why do-you-do that-which is not lawful on the sabbaths?"

**3.** And Jesus answering to them said, "Have-you-not-read even this, what David did when he hungered, he and those who were with him?

**4.** How he entered into the house of God and the loaves of the setting-forth he took and ate and gave to those with him, which is not lawful to eat except the priests alone?"

**5.** And he was saying to them, "The Son of Man is lord of the sabbath."

**6.** And it came-to-pass on another sabbath, he entered into the synagogue and was teaching. And there was a man there, and his right hand was withered.

**7.** And the scribes and the Pharisees were watching him closely, whether on the sabbath he heals, that they might find an accusation against him.

**8.** But he himself knew their reasonings, and he said to the man having the withered hand, "Rise and stand into the midst." And having risen, he stood.

**9.** And Jesus said to them, "I ask you whether it is lawful on the sabbath to do-good or to do-evil, to save a life or to destroy [it]?"

**10.** And having looked-around at all of them, he said to him, "Stretch-out your hand." And he did [so], and his hand was restored.

**11.** And they were filled with madness, and were discussing with one-another what they might do to Jesus.

**12.** And it came-to-pass in those days, he went-out to the mountain to pray, and he was spending-the-night in the prayer of God.

**13.** And when day came, he called his disciples, and having chosen from them twelve, whom also he named apostles:

**14.** Simon, whom also he named Peter, and Andrew his brother, and James and John, and Philip and Bartholomew,

**15.** and Matthew and Thomas, and James [son] of Alphaeus, and Simon the [one] being-called Zealot,

**16.** and Judas [son] of James, and Judas Iscariot, who became a betrayer.

**17.** And having come-down with them, he stood upon a level place, and a large crowd of his disciples, and a great multitude of the people from all Judea and Jerusalem and the coastal-region of Tyre and Sidon,

**18.** who came to hear him and to be healed from their diseases; and those being troubled by unclean spirits were being healed.

**19.** And all the crowd was seeking to touch him, because power was going-out from him and was healing all.

**20.** And he, having lifted-up his eyes upon his disciples, was saying, "Blessed [are] the poor, because yours is the kingdom of God.

**21.** Blessed [are] those hungering now, because you will be filled. Blessed [are] those weeping now, because you will laugh.

**22.** Blessed are-you when men hate you, and when they exclude you, and reproach [you], and cast-out your name as evil, on-account-of the Son of Man.

**23.** Rejoice in that day and leap, for behold, your reward [is] great in heaven; for according-to the same-things their fathers were doing to the prophets.

**24.** But woe to you, the rich, because you are receiving your consolation.

**25.** Woe to you who have been filled now, because you will hunger. Woe to you who are laughing now, because you will mourn and weep.

**26.** Woe when all men speak well of you, for according-to the same-things their fathers were doing to the false-prophets.

**27.** But to you I say, to those hearing: Love your enemies, do good to those hating you,

**28.** bless those cursing you, pray for those mistreating you.

**29.** To the [one] striking you upon the cheek, offer also the other; and from the [one] taking your outer-garment, also the tunic do not withhold.

**30.** To every [one] asking you, give; and from the [one] taking your [things], do not demand-back.

**31.** And just-as you wish that men should do to you, do to them likewise.

**32.** And if you love those loving you, what kind-of grace is [that] to you? For even the sinners love those loving them.

**33.** And if you do-good to those doing-good to you, what kind-of grace is [that] to you? For even the sinners do the same.

**34.** And if you lend [to those] from whom you hope to receive, what kind-of grace is [that] to you? Even sinners lend to sinners, that they might receive-back the equal [amount].

**35.** But love your enemies, and do-good, and lend hoping-for-nothing-back; and your reward will be great, and you will be sons of the Most-High, because he is kind toward the ungrateful and evil.

**36.** Be merciful, just-as your Father is merciful.

**37.** And do not judge, and you will by-no-means be judged; and do not condemn, and you will by-no-means be condemned. Release, and you will be released.

**38.** Give, and it will be given to you — good measure, pressed-down, shaken-together, overflowing, they will give into your bosom. For with what measure you measure, it will be measured-back to you.

**39.** And he spoke also a parable to them: "Is a blind-man able to lead a blind-man? Will not both fall into a pit?

**40.** A disciple is not above the teacher; but everyone having-been-fully-trained will be as his teacher.

**41.** And why do-you-look-at the speck that [is] in the eye of your brother, but the beam that [is] in your own eye you do not consider?

**42.** How are-you-able to say to your brother, 'Brother, let [me] cast-out the speck that [is] in your eye,' yourself not seeing the beam that [is] in your eye? Hypocrite, first cast-out the beam from your eye, and then you will see-clearly to cast-out the speck that [is] in the eye of your brother.

**43.** For there is not a good tree making rotten fruit, nor again a rotten tree making good fruit.

**44.** For each tree is known from its own fruit. For from thorns they do not gather figs, nor from a thorn-bush do-they-harvest grapes.

**45.** The good man from the good treasure of the heart brings-forth the good, and the evil [man] from the evil brings-forth the evil; for from [the] abundance of [the] heart his mouth speaks.

**46.** And why do-you-call me, 'Lord, Lord,' and do not do what I say?

**47.** Everyone coming to me and hearing my words and doing them — I will show you whom he is like:

**48.** He is like a man building a house, who dug and deepened and laid a foundation upon the rock; and a flood having come, the river burst-against that house, and was not strong-enough to shake it, because of its having been well built.

**49.** But the [one] having heard and not having done [them] is like a man having built a house upon the ground without a foundation, against which the river burst, and immediately it fell, and the ruin of that house was great."

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **6:1 — "on a sabbath"**: Sinaiticus reads εν σαββατω. Some manuscripts (including D) add the puzzling term δευτεροπρωτω ("second-first"), but Sinaiticus omits this entirely. The meaning of δευτεροπρωτω is debated and likely a later scribal gloss; Sinaiticus preserves the simpler reading.

2. **6:1 — "rubbing with [their] hands" (ψωχοντες ταις χερσιν)**: This detail is unique to Luke among the Synoptics. Sinaiticus preserves ψωχοντες, a rare verb meaning "to rub" — the action of rubbing grain husks off by hand. This constitutes the alleged "threshing" violation in the Pharisees' complaint.

3. **6:2 — "certain of the Pharisees"**: Sinaiticus reads τινες των Φαρισαιων, indicating it was not the whole group but some Pharisees who raised the objection.

4. **6:5 — "lord of the sabbath"**: Sinaiticus reads κυριος εστιν του σαββατου ο υιος του ανθρωπου. Some manuscripts (Codex Bezae, D) place after this verse an additional logion about a man working on the sabbath, but Sinaiticus contains no such addition.

5. **6:6 — "another sabbath" (ετερω σαββατω)**: Sinaiticus reads εν ετερω σαββατω, clearly distinguishing this event from the grain-field incident. The word ετερος ("another of a different kind") rather than αλλος is noteworthy.

6. **6:7 — "whether on the sabbath he heals"**: Sinaiticus reads ει εν τω σαββατω θεραπευει — the present tense θεραπευει ("heals") rather than a future or subjunctive, suggesting the Pharisees expected him to heal as a habitual action.

7. **6:9 — "to save a life or to destroy [it]"**: Sinaiticus reads ψυχην σωσαι η απολεσαι. The word ψυχη here means "life/soul" — Jesus frames the question in ultimate terms, not merely about healing but about preservation versus destruction.

8. **6:10 — "was restored" (απεκατεσταθη)**: Sinaiticus reads απεκατεσταθη — the aorist passive of αποκαθιστημι, "was restored." Some manuscripts add "whole as the other," but Sinaiticus omits this expansion.

9. **6:11 — "madness" (ανοιας)**: Sinaiticus reads ανοιας, literally "mindlessness/senselessness." This is a strong word — not merely anger but irrational fury. Luke is the only Synoptist to use this term here.

10. **6:12 — "in the prayer of God" (εν τη προσευχη του θεου)**: Sinaiticus preserves this unusual phrase. It could mean "in prayer to God" (objective genitive) or possibly "in the prayer-house of God" (προσευχη sometimes denoting a synagogue or place of prayer). EMET preserves the ambiguity of the Greek.

11. **6:15 — "Zealot" (Ζηλωτην)**: Sinaiticus reads τον καλουμενον Ζηλωτην. Luke uses the Greek form Ζηλωτης rather than the Aramaic-derived Κανανιτης ("Cananaean") found in Matthew/Mark, clarifying Simon's association with the zealot movement.

12. **6:16 — "Judas Iscariot"**: Sinaiticus reads Ιουδαν Ισκαριωθ. The form Ισκαριωθ (indeclinable) likely transliterates the Hebrew/Aramaic איש קריות ("man of Kerioth"), though other etymologies have been proposed.

13. **6:20 — "Blessed [are] the poor"**: Sinaiticus reads μακαριοι οι πτωχοι — simply "the poor," without Matthew's "in spirit" (τω πνευματι, Matt 5:3). Luke's Sermon on the Plain addresses material poverty directly. The second-person ημετερα ("yours") follows immediately, making the blessings directly addressed.

14. **6:22 — "cast-out your name as evil"**: Sinaiticus reads εκβαλωσιν το ονομα υμων ως πονηρον — literally "cast out your name as evil." This idiom means to defame or reject one's reputation.

15. **6:26 — "the false-prophets" (τοις ψευδοπροφηταις)**: Sinaiticus reads τοις ψευδοπροφηταις. The woes mirror the blessings: just as the prophets were persecuted (v. 23), the false-prophets were praised.

16. **6:29 — "outer-garment... tunic" (ιματιον... χιτωνα)**: Luke reverses the order found in Matthew 5:40. Matthew has χιτων then ιματιον (inner then outer garment, a legal seizure scenario); Luke has ιματιον then χιτων (outer then inner, a robbery scenario — someone seizing your cloak on the street).

17. **6:32-34 — "grace" (χαρις)**: Sinaiticus consistently reads χαρις ("grace/favor") in all three parallel questions, whereas Matthew 5:46-47 uses μισθος ("reward"). Luke's word choice carries theological weight — grace rather than merit.

18. **6:35 — "hoping-for-nothing-back" (μηδεν απελπιζοντες)**: Sinaiticus reads μηδεν απελπιζοντες. The verb απελπιζω is debated: it may mean "despairing of nothing" (i.e., never despairing) or "hoping for nothing back" (expecting no return). Context favors the latter reading, and EMET follows this sense.

19. **6:36 — "Be merciful" (γινεσθε οικτιρμονες)**: Sinaiticus reads γινεσθε οικτιρμονες, "become merciful/compassionate." Luke uses οικτιρμων ("compassionate") where Matthew 5:48 has τελειος ("perfect"). This is a significant theological variation between the Sermon on the Mount and the Sermon on the Plain.

20. **6:39 — "Is a blind-man able to lead a blind-man?"**: Sinaiticus reads μητι δυναται τυφλος τυφλον οδηγειν — expecting a negative answer ("surely not"). The parable form is a question in Sinaiticus, not a statement.

21. **6:40 — "having-been-fully-trained" (κατηρτισμενος)**: Sinaiticus reads κατηρτισμενος, the perfect passive participle of καταρτιζω ("to mend, complete, train fully"). The word implies thorough equipping — a disciple brought to completion.

22. **6:48 — "dug and deepened" (εσκαψεν και εβαθυνεν)**: Sinaiticus reads the double verb εσκαψεν και εβαθυνεν — "dug and deepened." This is Luke's distinctive expansion; Matthew simply says "built upon the rock." Luke emphasizes the deliberate effort of going deep.

23. **6:48 — "because of its having been well built"**: Sinaiticus reads δια το καλως οικοδομησθαι αυτην — "because of its having been well built." This differs from the Byzantine/Majority reading "for it had been founded upon the rock" (τεθεμελιωτο γαρ επι την πετραν). The Sinaiticus reading emphasizes the quality of construction rather than the foundation material.

24. **6:49 — "upon the ground" (επι την γην)**: Sinaiticus reads επι την γην, "upon the ground/earth," rather than "upon sand" (as in Matthew 7:26). Luke and Matthew use different imagery for the same point — Luke contrasts rock with bare earth, Matthew contrasts rock with sand.

25. **6:1 — Verse numbering**: The traditional verse division places the opening sabbath controversy at 6:1, continuing directly from the end of chapter 5. In Sinaiticus, there is no chapter or verse division; these are continuous text. The transition from the wineskins parable to the sabbath grain-field is unmarked.
